content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

Turner & Sullivan
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

JUN 23 9 55 AM '90

June 21, 1990

Kathryn E. Donovan, General Counsel
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

RE: Informal Assistance
Our File No. 157-88-30

Dear Ms. Donovan:

RICHARD K. TURNER
ROBERT J. SULLIVAN
JAMES P. CORN
DAVID L. KELLY
PEGGY A. CHRISTIANSEN
GRANT HERNDON
J. ANNE RAWLINS
LORI ANN JOSEPH

This firm represents the California Council of Civil Engineers
& Land Surveyors, a professional association of civil
engineering and land surveying firms located throughout the
State of California. As you know, we previously requested
and received your office's informal assistance regarding File
No. I-89-4131. We wish to thank you and your staff for the
work in providing that assistance.

While it was very helpful, it does not dispose of all the
issues. In fact, it raised a few additional issues which are
the subject of this request for further informal assistance.

The prior assistance request in effect assumed that all of the
work would be performed by registered civil engineers in
responsible charge of the various design work or benefit
spread formula work who are principals in the consulting
firms. No distinction was made as to the level of ownership
of the various engineers.

Assume, for a moment, that the other elements of Government
Code section 87100 are met, namely the "public official" and
the "participates in" elements, and that all of the work is
performed under the responsible charge of a registered civil
engineer who does not own a 10% interest in the consulting
engineering firm. Does that avoid the application of the
conflict of interest provisions? Assume further that the
consulting engineering firm received $250 or more in the
12 months prior to the decision, and that there is no material
financial effect on the consulting firm.

Your informal assistance response dated April 12, 1990, in
footnote 5 on page 5, discusses the application of Government
Code section 82030 and apparently concludes that if the
engineer has less than a 10% ownership interest in a firm, the

1755 CREEKSIDE OAKS DRIVE, SUITE 290, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95833
TELEPHONE 916/927-5057 FACSIMILE 916/927-5362

Kathryn E. Donovan, General Counsel
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
June 21, 1990
Page 2


disqualification obligations would apply only if the decision
had a material financial effect on the firm, rather than
clients of the firm. While we agree with that conclusion, it
certainly creates an anomalous situation.

Large firms that have many qualified registered civil
engineers or licensed land surveyors who may not own any
interest in the firm or, alternatively, who own less than a
10% interest, could avoid the disqualification provisions; but
smaller firms, where the only engineers who are appropriately
registered also are greater-than-10% owners, would be subject
to them. In effect, it discriminates against smaller firms
in performing such work.

Your informal assistance letter of April 12, 1990, on page 9,
third paragraph from the top, concludes that if a
subconsultant provides the design for the improvements and
prepares the estimates for the improvements, he participates
in the making of a governmental decision. Frankly, from a
common-sense perspective, it is the preparation of the benefit
spread formula that provides the obvious opportunity to favor
one client over a non-client. It really is difficult for the
engineering community to understand how the preparation of
various design services is likely to benefit a client to the
exclusion of a non-client.

In the prior request for informal assistance, we did not go
into great detail regarding the plan check requirement which
exists with respect to virtually all engineering design work.
When an engineer prepares plans and specifications, they are
reviewed by the plan check department of the public agency;
and, frankly, they frequently are very critically reviewed.
Why does the plan check requirement not satisfy the
"significant substantive review" requirements of 18700(c)(2)?

Similarly, the preparation of a boundary map for recordation
as part of the work for a benefit assessment district is going
to be subject to review prior to recordation. Similarly, the
assessment diagrams, which show the ownership of the various
individual parcels within the boundary of the proposed
assessment district, will be subject to review and approval.
In fact, even the construction cost estimates and the district
cost estimates are very likely to be scrutinized and reviewed
by the public agency. Another function performed by the
engineering firm is the posting and mailing of notices, as the
benefit assessment district approaches the public hearing
stage of the decision-making process.

Kathryn E. Donovan, General Counsel
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
June 21, 1990
Page 3


Frankly, it seems to us that so long as there is an
independent plan check or review, there is no "participation
in" the making of a governmental decision pursuant to the
cited regulation.

Alternatively, if the engineer structures his relationship
contractually, with the public agency so that he operates as
a true independent contractor and expressly adopts both of the
provisions of 18700(a)(2)(A) and (B), it seems that the
engineer can avoid being a "consultant" and, hence, avoid
being a "public official."

Would you please comment on the "participates in" regulation
as discussed above, and also whether or not the consultant can
contractually avoid being a "consultant" if he includes in his
contract both of the cited regulatory provisions.

Some engineering firms provide assessment district consulting
services and never have any formal contract with the public
agency. If there is no oral or written contract with the
public agency, rather all the work is done for the private
developer and paid for by the private developer, does that
avoid the application of the conflict of interest rules?
While the ability to favor a client over a non-client still
exists, it seems technically that engineer or engineering
firm would not be a "consultant" and, hence, not a "public
official" absent a contract with the public agency.

Again, let me emphasize our appreciation of the work that has
been done in rendering informal assistance at our request.
We also specifically would like to thank Jeevan Ahuja for
appearing at the seminars that recently have been conducted
by my client.

As you are aware, there is a great deal of interest in this
among the engineering community. While it technically has
been a problem since 1974, it largely was dormant until a
recent informal opinion of your office precipitated our
involvement. It substantially impacts the way engineers are
accustomed to doing business; and while my client is making
efforts to educate the engineering and surveying community
regarding the implications of the Fair Political Practices
Act, we also would like to continue to explore ways of
avoiding the problem.

Kathryn E. Donovan, General Counsel
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
June 21, 1990
Page 4


Thank you in advance for your continuing assistance.

Very truly yours,

TURNER & SULLIVAN
A Professional Corporation

[signature]

JAMES P. CORN

JPC/kb

CC:  Brad A. Kay
     Timothy G. Psomas
     James S. Mitchell
     Arlo J. Spiess
     Randy A. O'Dell
     John H. Heindel

sections.question_synthetic: The letter raises multiple follow-up questions regarding the application of Government Code section 87100 conflict of interest provisions to civil engineers and engineering firms performing assessment district work, including whether engineers with less than 10% ownership can avoid disqualification, whether independent plan check requirements satisfy the 'significant substantive review' standard, whether contractual provisions can avoid consultant/public official status, and whether lack of a formal contract with the public agency eliminates conflict of interest obligations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a law firm seeking FPPC guidance on conflict of interest issues, not an FPPC response. The document references a prior April 12, 1990 advice letter (File No. I-89-433) but that prior response is not included here. The current letter raises several distinct legal questions but does not follow a formal Q&A structure. Document is dated June 21, 1990.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The letter raises multiple follow-up questions regarding the application of Government Code section 87100 conflict of interest provisions to civil engineers and engineering firms performing assessment district work, including whether engineers with less than 10% ownership can avoid disqualification, whether independent plan check requirements satisfy the 'significant substantive review' standard, whether contractual provisions can avoid consultant/public official status, and whether lack of a formal contract with the public agency eliminates conflict of interest obligations.

embedding.summary: On behalf of the California Council of Civil Engineers & Land Surveyors, the law firm Turner & Sullivan submits an informal assistance request to the FPPC raising multiple follow-up questions about the application of conflict of interest rules to engineering firms performing assessment district consulting work, building on a previous advice letter.
